title: Picked Them Up in the 1870 Census
summary: Interviewed by Joe Mosnier in Albany, Georgia, in September 2011, Shirley Miller Sherrod opens on her family in Baker County, Georgia, whose roots she traced to the 1870 census, moving from sharecropping to buying land in what became known as the Hawkins family settlement, where her grandmother Hawkins and grandfather Julius Miller began with 31 acres and left each of their children at least 31 acres.

title: The Gator Ran Baker County
summary: Sherrod describes Baker County's reputation and Sheriff L. Warren "Gator" Johnson, who controlled everything in the county and ran a speed trap where a Black driver could pay or lose his life, and recalls her first segregated school in army barracks before a new building was built as Georgia's answer to separate but equal.

title: He Wanted a Son
summary: As the oldest of five girls, Sherrod recalls her father wanting a son and giving each daughter a boy's nickname, calling her Bill, and learning from her best friend that her mother was pregnant again because he had handed out cigars at the store, a baby who would be born two months after he was murdered, and her family's central place in their church.

title: The Cucumber Stain on Your Hands
summary: She contrasts children whose families owned land with those living on plantations like Robert Woodruff's Ichauway and the Mellon family's Pineland, and remembers being picked up from school on cucumber days to go home and pick, a hard task she came to see as a source of pride in working land her family owned.

part: I Have to Get Away From This
title: The Superintendent's Wife Took Over
summary: Sherrod recalls the worst buses that broke down and caught fire and the hand-me-down textbooks with torn-out pages from the white school, and the day the superintendent's wife walked into her French class, made the teacher feel like nothing, and drilled the students until they pronounced words with a Southern twang, sharpening her resolve to leave the South.

part: I Have to Get Away From This
title: The Black Dentist and the Movement
summary: She describes traveling only as far as Texas through the New Homemakers of America, the segregated parallel to the white Future Homemakers, and her family's awareness of the Albany Movement: members stopping at her church to take up collections, and a Black dentist in Albany who talked with her father about eventually getting the movement started in Baker County.

part: I Have to Get Away From This
title: They Bombed the Houses
summary: Sherrod recounts her father deciding he should register to vote as a taxpayer, and earlier efforts in the 1950s by relatives including Uncle Josh, Benjamin T. Coney, Ike King, and Carl Broadway, whose houses and cars were bombed and on whose place moonshine was planted, and how a Black man was told he could only borrow to build a house of blocks or wood, never brick.

title: He Came to Get the Cows
summary: Sherrod describes her father's happiness in early 1965 with a new baby expected and a son's room being built, and recounts the morning a white farmer named Cal Hall stopped them on the way to church about a cow, leading to a meeting at the pasture the next day where Cal Hall tried to claim five or six cows that were not his.

title: We'll Just Go to Court
topic: The Murder and the Grand Jury
summary: She tells how, when her father said they would settle the cattle argument in court and turned toward his truck, he was shot, lingering ten days before he died on March 25, 1965, and how the white grand jury refused to indict, a later civil trial in which attorney C.B. King exposed jurors' contempt for Black testimony still ending in the verdict they expected.

title: A Cross Burned at the House
summary: Sherrod recounts confronting the Gator while trying to register and being pushed out of the courthouse, then how an uncle from Calhoun helped her enroll at Fort Valley State after her father's death left the family without support, and the call two weeks in that white men had burned a cross at the house, surrounded by armed Black men the movement had organized.

title: You Can Stay in the South to Work for Change
topic: The Night of Her Father's Death
summary: She describes the night of her father's death, going alone into a room to pray as the house filled with mourners, and the sudden calm that came when she resolved to give up her dream of the North and stay in the South to work for change, a commitment that became clear at her first mass meeting watching plantation people refuse to be afraid.

part: The Movement Comes to Baker County
title: Five Carloads to Washington
topic: The Trip to Testify in Washington
summary: Sherrod names early movement figures including Charles Sherrod and recounts planning a Washington trip to testify before Congress and march at the Justice Department about Baker County, leaving Thankful Baptist Church in a caravan that routed east through Mitchell County to evade the Gator, with Aunt Josie talking her way past a state trooper by saying she was taking children to see the White House, and reflects on the Justice Department's limits and the injunction the summer's work finally won against the Gator.

part: The Movement Comes to Baker County
title: The Whites Did Not Have to Leave
summary: Sherrod describes returning to enroll at Albany State around 1967 to be near the work, and how when Stokely Carmichael took over SNCC from John Lewis and said all whites had to leave under the Black power movement, she and Charles Sherrod disagreed, broke with SNCC, and incorporated their own organization to keep the white workers, the Southwest Georgia Project.

part: The Movement Comes to Baker County
title: They Set the House on Fire
summary: She paints Reverend Charles Sherrod as fearless despite beatings and threats, recounts marrying him about a year after they met in 1967, and tells how, while printing a newsletter, white men took a stuttering boy named Grady Christian and set their house on fire, after which a flat tire on the dark road to Baker County left them at the mercy of white men who, not recognizing Sherrod, changed the tire.

part: The Movement Comes to Baker County
title: He Spoke, Then Took It Back
summary: Sherrod describes her mother's fear yet growing strength, and her shock when, eleven years after her husband's murder, her mother ran for office against the backdrop of the Gator's retirement and his son Scrut's campaign, recounting an election-night moment when the Gator, realizing he had greeted Charles Sherrod, took it back and stared him down with a gun on his hip until his son pulled him away.

part: The Movement Comes to Baker County
title: He Refused to Leave
topic: A Chance to Move to Kentucky
summary: She recalls a hard stretch around the late 1980s after the loss of the land at New Communities, when a friend who directed the Presbyterian Hunger Program suggested Charles Sherrod take a church job in Louisville, Kentucky, where Sherrod could have continued her Federation work, and how, though no one locally would hire him because of his civil rights history, he refused to leave, saying he had work to do in the area.

title: Seven People Went to Israel
summary: Sherrod explains how families kicked off white-owned land for enrolling children in white schools led to the idea of building a community, with seven people studying the Israeli kibbutz and returning with a model that became New Communities, an ambitious plan to buy land across the country, beginning with a 6,000-acre plot, and a planning grant from the Office of Economic Opportunity.

title: We Could Dream the Community
summary: She describes the charrette sessions where future residents and experts planned housing, villages, education, health, industry, and a rail spur, and the committee structure for the farm, run not as a plantation but as something new and equal, where she and Charles Sherrod deliberately did not take the big house, holding the land as a trust owned not by him or her but by all of them.

title: The Money From the Government
summary: Sherrod recounts how Slater King, C.B. King's brother, arranged interim financing on the expectation of federal money, but when Governor Lester Maddox blocked the OEO funds the project faced foreclosure, held together for years by Prudential's first mortgage and creative financing, and explains the choice of the Moshav rather than the kibbutz model, since the kibbutz kept children from their families.

title: Way Ahead of Our Time
summary: She describes the innovations at New Communities that others tried to use to divide them by warning residents they would own nothing: muscadine grapes no one else grew in the 1970s, a 75-brood-sow operation, and a farmers' market on Highway 19 with an old-fashioned smokehouse and sugar cane mill known for cured meats and sugar cane syrup.

part: The Federation and the Land Loss Fight
title: Learn Everything You Can Learn
summary: Sherrod tells how, needing skills New Communities could not pay for, she took night business classes at Albany State toward an MBA through Valdosta State, organized the first day care center in Baker, and pulled farmers together to elect the first Black person to the ASCS committee by dividing up the voter list, learning to do everything herself while working.

part: The Federation and the Land Loss Fight
title: A Loan Over My Dead Body
summary: She recounts the three-year fight for emergency farm money after droughts beginning around 1976, when a county supervisor said they would get a loan over his dead body, and how farmers home administration tied up all of New Communities' available assets, four and a half million in worth, before moving in for the kill, leading her to apply to the Rural Development Leadership Network to pursue a master's.

part: The Federation and the Land Loss Fight
title: They Pushed the Buildings Over
summary: Sherrod describes attending the Leadership Network institute at UC Davis from May to June 1985, missing her daughter's graduation but avoiding the courthouse sale, joining the Federation of Southern Cooperatives' staff that July, and how after the land sold in September the new owner dug holes and pushed the houses, smokehouse, and sugar cane mill into them, leaving her to pour everything into helping other Black farmers.

title: Will You Put That in Writing
summary: Working the issue of land loss with the Federation, Sherrod recounts learning the regulations better than the agency, and a confrontation in Blakely, Georgia, where she stopped a county supervisor threatening to foreclose on Marvel Smith's farm and home, demanding he put it in writing until he refused, a fight that helped push the Credit Act of 1987 and eventually drove the supervisor from the agency.

title: The Slick Lawyer Out of Texas
summary: Sherrod describes deciding with the Federation, the Land Loss Prevention Project, and Farmers' Legal Action Group to file suit, first to force USDA to produce complaint records a judge threatened jail to obtain, then preparing six test cases that a lawyer out of Texas pulled away by signing the farmers to a retainer, and the choice the Pigford class faced between trial and settling given many farmers lacked full documentation.

title: Track A and Track B
summary: She explains the settlement's two classes: Track A claimants needed little proof and would receive fifty thousand dollars, though the government later taxed the whole amount and wrote off debt only for the single cited instance of discrimination, while Track B farmers with more documentation got a day in court, and how of roughly 22,000 claimants who heard in time, only about 180 filed Track B in what she calls Pigford One.

title: We Could File a Claim
summary: Sherrod recounts the night, driving from Eufaula, Alabama, when she suddenly realized that because New Communities was farming in 1981 it too could file a Pigford claim, rushing home to tell her husband, and how in the Lee County courthouse they finally saw the documentation that big plantations had been lent the money denied to them, the buyer who paid a million for their land lent $950,000 three weeks later.

title: The Wife of the Chief Adjudicator
summary: She describes the 2002 hearing where the hearing officer was the wife of Pigford's chief adjudicator, an incompetent DOJ lawyer who, when the farmers home witness began telling the truth, called a break and brought him back lying, and how, though they appeared to win in the room, in October they received a letter ruling against them, which Sherrod attributes to politics over the cost of early Pigford payouts.

title: She Said It's Twelve
summary: Sherrod recounts appealing to the monitor, a friend who recused herself, and a reviewer who spent four years producing a fifty- or sixty-page document on the miscarriage of justice, then the chief adjudicator's three-year silence until a July 2009 call telling her they had won twelve million dollars, a decision she says she cries over each time she reads its point-by-point reasoning.

title: Why Would We Want an Antebellum Home
summary: With the award the dream continued, and Sherrod tells of looking at a hunting place before a second property with an antebellum home built in 1851 and restored at a cost said to be three million, which she opposed while her husband and others favored it, leading her to bring in an outside expert who recommended the antebellum place over the one she preferred.

title: The Wealthiest Slave Owner in Georgia
summary: She recounts negotiating the price from a listed 21 million down to 4.5 million with equipment for the place now called Cypress Pond Plantation, and learning it had belonged to the wealthiest slave owner in Georgia, who held about a thousand slaves across three places and 150 there, and that Jefferson Davis stayed there planning Civil War activities, a history of layered irony.

title: It Was More About Being Poor
summary: Asked to reflect, Sherrod tells how her work led white farmers to seek her help, the Spooners being the first white family, and how, after working through what happened to her father in order to help a white farmer, she came to see the issue as more about being poor than about race, a lesson she has spent twenty-five years urging people in the area to act on by working together.

title: You Have to Accept Training
summary: She recounts organizing a Baker County feeder-pig cooperative whose farmers insisted they already knew how to raise hogs, and the visit with the Heifer Project man to a farmer who bragged he raised his on concrete, where they found hogs amid broken glass and contaminated water and crippled pigs under a peanut trailer, after which she started the training herself and never had the problem again.

title: They Said It Was His Story
topic: Ben and Jerry's and the Credit
summary: Sherrod describes the commercial kitchen and Head Start she built in the old 1957 school building, the Ben and Jerry's effort to fight Black land loss by buying pecans, where processors refused to handle Black farmers' nuts until the company found its own supplier, and how she had farmers save half the premium toward a women-run facility while a man named Rapunzel Page was widely credited for work that was hers, closing on the present effort to bring more women back into agriculture as the session ends.
